comments.text: Based on this MO discussion the problem is independent modulo large cardinals. More precisely, the positive answer holds in the constructible universe $L$ so is consistent. The negative answer is consistent assuming the consistency of something called rank-into-rank axiom, which is pretty high in the large cardinal hierarchy; the exact large cardinal needed is probably open, but it's at least zero sharp. I would also like to propose distinguishing problems like this from problems that are independent but don't involve large cardinals, namely #1119 and #1127.

GPT-5.4 Pro came up with a nice argument involving Magidor cardinals to get yes/no classification, it does however need to assume certain large cardinals hypotheses, making it still open in ZFC. Here's a note and here's its formalization in Lean by Aristotle.

Congrats. I ran standard check which claimed some issues related to citation (but nonmathematical). Regarding Lean, it also claimed there were slight mismatches of the assumed statements and the literature; perhaps easily fixable though.

I revisit this briefly in the hope that the mismatches can be easily bridged. It turns out there is a bigger issue in that Theorem 4.3 is not formalized at all: theorem threshold_model_description : True := trivial with the excuse: Since forcing extensions cannot be directly expressed in Lean's type theory, this result is recorded here as documentation. So I can't easily verify the result from the Lean file. Credit to GPT-5.5 Thinking for discussion. This also adds another example to the ongoing discourse on how you can fumble when collaborating with AI on real or complex (no pun intended) tasks.

I think the statement is true in the special case \(m=\kappa=(2^{\aleph_0})^+\). Indeed, let\[ E^\kappa_\omega=\{\delta<\kappa:\operatorname{cf}(\delta)=\omega\}. \]Since \(\kappa\) is a regular uncountable cardinal, \(E^\kappa_\omega\) is stationary in \(\kappa\). Also, a stationary subset of a regular uncountable cardinal can be partitioned into \(\kappa\) many pairwise disjoint stationary sets. So fix a partition\[ E^\kappa_\omega=\bigsqcup_{\xi<\kappa} S_\xi \]with each \(S_\xi\) stationary. Now define a colouring\[ c:[\kappa]^{\aleph_0}\to\kappa \]by\[ c(a)=\xi \iff \sup(a)\in S_\xi. \]Now let \(X\subseteq\kappa\) with \(|X|=\kappa\). Since \(\kappa\) is regular, \(X\) is unbounded in \(\kappa\). Hence the set of limit points of \(X\) is club in \(\kappa\). For any \(\xi<\kappa\), because \(S_\xi\) is stationary, there is some\[ \delta\in S_\xi \]which is a limit point of \(X\). Since \(\operatorname{cf}(\delta)=\omega\), we can choose a countable subset \(a\subseteq X\cap\delta\) cofinal in \(\delta\). Then\[ \sup(a)=\delta, \]so by definition of the colouring,\[ c(a)=\xi. \]As \(\xi<\kappa\) was arbitrary, every \(X\in[\kappa]^\kappa\) contains countable subsets of all \(\kappa\) colours. Therefore the required property holds for \(m=\kappa=(2^{\aleph_0})^+\). Disclosure: the main idea of this argument, namely colouring a countable set by the stationary piece containing its supremum after partitioning \(E^\kappa_\omega\) into \(\kappa\) stationary sets, was suggested with the help of AI; I then wrote out the proof in this form.

ChatGPT Pro indicates some (likely fixable) issues with the argument, and also points out that this partial result already follows from an existing result of Garti and Hayut. It claims that the main difficulty in the problem comes from the case when $m$ is large. Somani's site has a DeepResearch review of the problem. Apparently the expert belief is that this problem is independent of ZFC.
